description:
You are a network engineer who has just received a connection-health report generated by an old monitoring appliance.  
The appliance writes its report in UTF-16 LE with a BOM, while your team’s tool-chain expects plain UTF-8.  
Your job is to:

1. Convert the file /home/user/network/logs/connection_report_utf16le.txt from UTF-16 LE (with BOM) to UTF-8 **without** a BOM.  
2. Save the converted file as /home/user/network/logs/connection_report_utf8.txt.  
3. Create a second file, /home/user/network/logs/failed_connections.log, that contains **only** the lines whose STATUS field is the word “FAILED”.  

Output & format requirements the automated grader will check:

A. /home/user/network/logs/connection_report_utf8.txt  
   • Encoding: UTF-8, no BOM.  
   • Exactly four lines, each terminated by a single LF (hex 0A) and **no CR (0D)**.  
   • The lines must appear **in the same order** as the source file:  
     1) 2023-07-21 10:12:05 192.168.1.10 OK  
     2) 2023-07-21 10:14:27 192.168.1.11 FAILED  
     3) 2023-07-21 10:15:32 192.168.1.12 OK  
     4) 2023-07-21 10:17:43 192.168.1.13 FAILED  

B. /home/user/network/logs/failed_connections.log  
   • Encoding: UTF-8, no BOM.  
   • Exactly two lines, each terminated by a single LF (hex 0A) and no CR.  
   • The file must contain ONLY these lines, in this order:  
     1) 2023-07-21 10:14:27 192.168.1.11 FAILED  
     2) 2023-07-21 10:17:43 192.168.1.13 FAILED  

Hints you might consider (do NOT reveal exact commands):
• A standard Linux utility exists specifically for character-set conversion.  
• Any basic text-filtering tool can extract the lines you need once the file is UTF-8.  

No additional files should be modified or created. The grading script will verify the exact file paths, encodings and line content described above.
